sanskrit_devanagari: शनै: शनैरुपरमेद्‍बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया ।आत्मसंस्थं मन: कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत् ॥ २५ ॥
sanskrit_iast: śanaiḥ śanair uparamedbuddhyā dhṛti-gṛhītayāātma-saṁsthaṁ manaḥ kṛtvāna kiñcid api cintayet

translations.verbatim_quote: Gradually, step by step, one should become situated in trance by means of intelligence sustained by full conviction, and thus the mind should be fixed on the Self alone and should think of nothing else.

Gradually, step by step, one should become situated in trance by means of intelligence sustained by full conviction, and thus the mind should be fixed on the self alone and should think of nothing else.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 623 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: By proper conviction and intelligence one should gradually cease sense activities. This is called pratyāhāra. The mind, being controlled by conviction, meditation and cessation from the senses, should be situated in trance, or samādhi.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.24 – 6.25 There are two kinds of desires: 1) those born of contact between the senses and objects like heat, cold etc.; 2) those generated by our mind (will) like that for sons, land etc. Of these, the latter type of desires are by their own nature relinquishable. Relinquishing all these
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-25-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)

positions.rendering: Gradually, step by step, one should become situated in trance
by degrees, with intellect endowed with steady firmness, one should withdraw
relinquish desires born of sense-contact and of mental willing; restrain senses by mind

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All traditions read uparamet gradualistically (śanaiḥ śanaiḥ — by degrees, by degrees). Shankara emphasises buddhi held by dhṛti as the instrument; Ramanuja (treating 6.24-25 as a paired unit) emphasises which desires are relinquishable. The gradualism is uncontroversial across traditions.

literal_meaning: Gradually, by degrees, one should arrest (the mind), with intellect held by firmness; making the mind fixed on the Self, one should not think of anything at all.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.25 names the gradual method of mental restraint. Śanaiḥ śanaiḥ — gradually, by degrees — uparamet, should withdraw the mind. The instrument is buddhi (intellect) held by dhṛti (firmness). The result-state is manaḥ ātma-saṁstham — the mind fixed on the Self — and na kiñcid api cintayet, do not think of anything else. Shankara: with intellect endowed with steady firmness one should withdraw the mind by degrees. Ramanuja (paired 6.24-25): two kinds of desires must be relinquished — those born of sense-contact and those generated by mental willing — and the senses restrained on every side by mind. The doctrine is gradualist; the chapter does not promise the result is achieved in a single sitting. STRETCHED at full metaphysical scope; partial engineering analog at gradual-discipline scope.
